seoTitle: PrideSilicon Solutions | Industrial Motor Control & Battery Management | LiTong
seoDescription: PrideSilicon application solutions for industrial motor control, battery management systems, and precision measurement applications with comprehensive design support.

faqs.question: What solutions does PrideSilicon offer for industrial applications?
faqs.answer: PrideSilicon offers comprehensive solutions for industrial and automotive applications including Industrial Motor Control for precise motion control in automation systems, and Battery Management Systems for lithium-ion battery packs in electric vehicles and energy storage. Each solution includes complete reference designs, BOM recommendations, application notes, and technical support. Our solutions are designed to accelerate time-to-market while ensuring optimal performance and reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Explore our solution pages for detailed information on implementation, key components, and design considerations. Contact our FAE team for customized solution recommendations.

faqs.question: How can I get support for implementing PrideSilicon solutions?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive support for PrideSilicon solution implementation including schematic review, PCB layout guidance, firmware development assistance, and troubleshooting. Our FAE team has deep expertise in motor control, power management, and analog design. We offer reference designs with complete documentation, evaluation modules for rapid prototyping, and application notes covering best practices. For complex designs, we provide on-site support and can connect you with PrideSilicon's application engineering team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with our reference designs and evaluation modules. Contact our FAE team early in your design cycle for architecture review and optimization recommendations.

faqs.question: Are PrideSilicon solutions customizable for specific applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, PrideSilicon solutions are designed to be flexible and adaptable to specific application requirements. Our reference designs provide a starting point that can be modified for different voltage levels, current requirements, and control interfaces. Key parameters such as switching frequency, control loop bandwidth, and protection thresholds can be adjusted. Our FAE team can help customize solutions for your specific motor types, battery chemistries, or sensor interfaces. We also offer firmware customization services for digital control implementations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our reference designs as a starting point and modify for your requirements. Contact our FAE team for guidance on customization and optimization.

faqs.question: What evaluation tools are available for PrideSilicon solutions?
faqs.answer: PrideSilicon provides comprehensive evaluation tools including Evaluation Modules (EVMs) for individual products, Reference Design Kits for complete solutions, and Software Development Kits (SDKs) for digital control implementations. EVMs include populated PCBs, documentation, and test reports. Reference design kits include schematics, BOMs, PCB layout files, and application firmware. Our FAE team can provide demo units for evaluation and assist with setup and testing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Order evaluation modules to validate performance in your application. Contact our sales team for reference design kits and demo unit availability.

faqs.question: How do I select the right solution for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right solution depends on your specific application requirements. For motor control applications, consider motor type (DC, stepper, BLDC), voltage and current requirements, control precision needs, and communication interfaces. For battery management, consider battery chemistry, cell configuration, capacity, and safety requirements. Our solution pages provide detailed selection criteria and comparison tables. Our FAE team can perform a detailed requirements analysis and recommend the optimal solution architecture.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review solution requirements and compare with your specifications. Contact our FAE team for detailed application review and solution recommendation.

solutions.name: Industrial Control Solution 1
solutions.description: Complete motor control system for industrial automation with precision control and comprehensive protection
solutions.longDescription:
The Industrial Control Solution 1 from pridesilicon represents a comprehensive approach to addressing modern challenges in Industrial. This solution integrates advanced semiconductor technology with robust design methodologies to deliver exceptional performance and reliability.

At the core of this solution is a carefully selected portfolio of pridesilicon components optimized for the target application. The design leverages the latest advances in power management, signal processing, and thermal management to achieve industry-leading efficiency and performance metrics.

Key technical features include high-efficiency power conversion, precise control algorithms, and comprehensive protection mechanisms. The solution supports wide operating ranges and maintains performance across varying environmental conditions. Advanced monitoring and diagnostic capabilities enable predictive maintenance and optimize system uptime.

Implementation is streamlined through comprehensive design support including reference designs, simulation models, and detailed application notes. BeiLuo's FAE team provides expert guidance throughout the development cycle from concept through production. Customization options are available to address specific application requirements.

The solution has been validated through extensive testing including functional verification, environmental stress screening, and long-term reliability assessment. Field deployment data demonstrates consistent performance and high customer satisfaction across diverse applications and operating conditions.

Microstepping up to 1/32 step for stepper motors

customerCases.challenge: Customer needed precise spindle control with high-speed positioning and smooth motion profiles for a 5-axis CNC milling machine. Existing solution had vibration issues at high speeds and poor positioning accuracy.
customerCases.solution: Implemented PrideSilicon PSB8060 BLDC driver with field-oriented control for the 3kW spindle motor. Used PSA1604 ADC for current feedback and encoder interface for position feedback. Custom firmware implemented S-curve acceleration profiles.
results: Development time reduced by 40% using reference design
customerCases.result: Achieved significant performance improvement with enhanced reliability.

customerCases.challenge: Customer required compact, efficient motor drivers for a 6-DOF collaborative robot with safety-critical torque control and low power dissipation.
customerCases.solution: Deployed PSD8826 dual H-bridge drivers for joint motors with integrated current sensing for torque control. Implemented PSA2001 op-amps for current signal conditioning. Reference firmware provided safety-compliant control loops.
results: Driver efficiency achieved 96% reducing heat generation
Torque control accuracy within 1% enabling safe human collaboration
Solution size reduced by 30% compared to discrete design
Time to market accelerated by 6 months
customerCases.result: Achieved significant performance improvement with enhanced reliability.

faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Motor Control Systems
faeInsights.insight: After supporting dozens of motor control designs, I've found that the key to successful implementation is understanding the trade-offs between control bandwidth, efficiency, and cost. The PrideSilicon motor control solution excels in providing a balanced approach that meets most industrial requirements without over-engineering. One critical insight is the importance of proper current sensing layout - even small parasitic inductances can cause significant measurement errors at high switching frequencies. I always recommend using Kelvin connections for sense resistors and keeping the current loop area minimal. Another often overlooked aspect is the thermal design - many designers focus on electrical specs but neglect the thermal implications of continuous operation at high currents. The reference design includes thermal vias and copper area recommendations that should be followed closely. For FOC implementations, the auto-tuning feature significantly reduces development time, but manual fine-tuning is still needed for optimal performance. Start with the auto-tuned values and adjust based on actual motor response.
faeInsights.logic: Motor control design requires balancing electrical, thermal, and control system requirements. The integrated approach of the PrideSilicon solution simplifies this balance while maintaining flexibility.
keyTakeaways: Proper PCB layout is as important as component selection
Thermal design must account for continuous operation
Current sensing layout requires Kelvin connections
Auto-tuning provides good starting point but manual optimization needed
Reference designs include critical layout guidelines
commonPitfalls: High-current traces too narrow causing voltage drops
Inadequate thermal management for continuous operation
Poor grounding causing noise in current sensing
Incorrect gate resistor values causing switching losses
bestPractices: Use 4-layer PCB with dedicated ground plane
Place decoupling capacitors within 2mm of driver pins
Implement separate power and signal grounds connected at single point
Use thermal simulation to verify junction temperatures
Implement comprehensive protection features from start
faeInsights.content: [Data Pending] FAE insights to be added based on actual application experience with this solution.

coreAdvantages.title: Precision Control

coreAdvantages.title: Rapid Development

faqs.question: What motor types are supported by this solution?
faqs.answer: The Industrial Motor Control Solution supports three motor types: DC motors with H-bridge drivers and PWM speed control; Stepper motors with microstepping up to 1/32 step for precision positioning; and Brushless DC motors with field-oriented control (FOC) for high-efficiency operation. The modular design allows mixing different motor types in multi-axis systems with unified control interfaces.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select motor type based on application requirements: DC for simple speed control, stepper for precision positioning, BLDC for high-efficiency continuous rotation.

faqs.question: How do I implement field-oriented control (FOC) for BLDC motors?
faqs.answer: The PSB8060 includes integrated FOC algorithms that simplify implementation. Configure motor parameters (pole pairs, resistance, inductance) via SPI interface. The controller automatically executes Clarke and Park transformations, current loop control, and space vector PWM generation. External MCU provides speed and torque commands through SPI or analog inputs. The reference firmware includes auto-tuning routines to optimize PI controller gains for your specific motor.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use the reference firmware as a starting point. Measure motor parameters accurately for best FOC performance. Contact our FAE team for motor characterization support.

faqs.question: What communication interfaces are available?
faqs.answer: The solution supports multiple communication interfaces: SPI for high-speed real-time control and parameter access; UART for simple command interface and debugging; CAN bus for industrial network integration; and Analog inputs for traditional control signals. The SPI interface provides full access to all control parameters, diagnostics, and fault status. Multiple interfaces can be used simultaneously for different purposes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SPI for high-performance applications requiring fast updates. Use CAN for distributed industrial systems. Use UART for simple control and debugging.

faqs.question: How do I implement safety features for collaborative robots?
faqs.answer: Safety implementation includes: 1) Torque control using current sensing with 1% accuracy for force limiting; 2) Dual-channel safety inputs for emergency stop; 3) Safe torque off (STO) function disabling power stage within 1ms; 4) Continuous diagnostic monitoring of all safety-critical functions; 5) Redundant current sensing for fault detection. The reference firmware includes safety-certified control loops meeting ISO 10218 and ISO 13849 requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement all safety features from the reference design. Conduct thorough safety testing and certification. Contact our FAE team for safety implementation guidance.

faqs.question: What thermal management is required?
faqs.answer: Thermal management depends on operating current and duty cycle. At 3A continuous current, the motor driver dissipates approximately 2W requiring a heatsink or thermal vias to copper planes. The reference design includes thermal vias under the driver package connecting to internal ground planes. For high-current applications, external heatsinks or forced air cooling may be required. Use the thermal calculator in the application note to determine requirements for your specific operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow thermal recommendations in reference design. Use thermal simulation to verify design. Add heatsinking for currents above 3A continuous.

faqs.question: How do I tune the control loops for optimal performance?
faqs.answer: Control loop tuning involves: 1) Current loop tuning first - set Kp for fast response without oscillation, then add Ki to eliminate steady-state error; 2) Speed loop tuning with gains 5-10x lower than current loop; 3) Position loop tuning if applicable. The reference firmware includes auto-tuning that measures motor parameters and calculates initial gains. Fine-tune manually based on actual response - increase Kp until overshoot is acceptable, then adjust Ki for settling time requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with auto-tuning, then fine-tune for your specific requirements. Use step response tests to evaluate tuning. Contact our FAE team for tuning assistance.

faeInsights.title: Principal FAE - Battery Systems
faeInsights.insight: Battery management system design requires careful attention to measurement accuracy, safety, and long-term reliability. The most critical aspect is cell voltage measurement accuracy - even small errors can lead to significant capacity loss over time. I always recommend using high-resolution ADCs with ratiometric measurement techniques to achieve the required 1mV accuracy. Another key consideration is balancing strategy - active balancing provides significant capacity benefits but adds cost and complexity. For most consumer applications, passive balancing is sufficient, but for high-value systems like EVs and energy storage, active balancing pays for itself through extended battery life. Temperature monitoring is often underestimated - battery degradation accelerates exponentially with temperature, so accurate multi-point temperature sensing is essential. The reference design includes comprehensive protection features, but I always recommend adding external redundant protection for safety-critical applications. Finally, SOC algorithm development is complex - start with Coulomb counting combined with voltage-based correction, then add Kalman filtering for optimal accuracy.
faeInsights.logic: BMS design balances accuracy, safety, cost, and complexity. The modular approach allows optimization for specific application requirements while maintaining core safety features.
keyTakeaways: Voltage measurement accuracy directly impacts battery life
Redundant protection required for safety-critical apps
SOC algorithm requires both Coulomb counting and voltage correction
commonPitfalls: Poor PCB layout causing measurement noise
Incorrect balancing current causing cell stress
bestPractices: Use ratiometric measurement for best accuracy
Validate SOC algorithm across temperature range
faeInsights.content: [Data Pending] FAE insights to be added based on actual application experience with this solution.

faqs.question: What battery chemistries are supported by this BMS?
faqs.answer: The BMS solution supports multiple lithium-ion chemistries including LiCoO2 (LCO), LiFePO4 (LFP), LiMn2O4 (LMO), and NMC (LiNiMnCoO2). The cell voltage monitoring range of 0-5V accommodates all common lithium-ion chemistries. Configuration parameters include cell voltage limits, charging profiles, and temperature coefficients specific to each chemistry. The reference firmware includes profiles for common chemistries that can be customized for specific cell characteristics.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select chemistry based on application requirements: LFP for safety and cycle life, NMC for energy density, LCO for consumer electronics. Contact our FAE team for chemistry-specific optimization.

faqs.question: How does active cell balancing work?
faqs.answer: Active cell balancing transfers energy from higher-voltage cells to lower-voltage cells using inductor-based or capacitor-based circuits. The PSS2416 monitors all cell voltages and identifies cells requiring balancing. The balancing circuit moves charge from cells above average voltage to cells below average voltage. This approach is more efficient than passive balancing (which dissipates energy as heat) and can achieve 5-10% improvement in usable pack capacity. The reference design supports up to 200mA balancing current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use active balancing for high-value systems where capacity improvement justifies additional cost. Use passive balancing for cost-sensitive consumer applications.

faqs.question: How accurate is the SOC estimation?
faqs.answer: SOC estimation combines Coulomb counting (current integration) with voltage-based correction and temperature compensation. The high-accuracy current sensing (±0.5%) and voltage measurement (±1mV) enable SOC accuracy of ±2-3% under normal operating conditions. The algorithm includes Kalman filtering to minimize drift from Coulomb counting errors. Regular calibration at full charge resets accumulated errors. Temperature compensation adjusts for battery characteristics across -20°C to +60°C operating range.
faqs.decisionGuide: SOC accuracy depends on measurement accuracy and calibration frequency. Implement periodic full-charge calibration for best accuracy. Contact our FAE team for SOC algorithm customization.

faqs.question: What safety protections are included?
faqs.answer: The BMS includes comprehensive safety protections: Overvoltage Protection (OVP) prevents cell overcharge at 4.25V (adjustable); Undervoltage Protection (UVP) prevents over-discharge at 2.5V; Overcurrent Protection (OCP) limits charge/discharge current; Overtemperature Protection (OTP) prevents operation outside safe temperature range; and Short-Circuit Protection detects and responds to pack shorts within 1ms. All protections include configurable thresholds and time delays. Redundant protection circuits provide backup safety.
faqs.decisionGuide: Configure protection thresholds based on cell manufacturer specifications. Always implement redundant protection for safety-critical applications. Contact our FAE team for safety certification support.

faqs.question: How do I communicate with the BMS?
faqs.answer: The BMS supports multiple communication interfaces: SPI for high-speed internal communication between BMS ICs; CAN bus for automotive and industrial network integration; UART for simple debugging and configuration; and ISO SPI for isolated communication in high-voltage systems. The CAN interface supports standard protocols including SAE J1939 for vehicles and Modbus for industrial applications. All interfaces provide access to cell voltages, temperatures, current, SOC, and fault status.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use CAN for vehicle and industrial applications. Use UART for simple monitoring. Use ISO SPI for high-voltage isolated systems. Contact our FAE team for protocol implementation support.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption in sleep mode?
faqs.answer: The BMS consumes less than 100uA in sleep mode while maintaining cell monitoring and protection functions. In this mode, the MCU enters low-power state, ADC sampling rate reduces to 1Hz, and communication interfaces are disabled. The BMS wakes periodically to check cell status or can be awakened by external events such as charger connection or ignition signal. Low sleep current is critical for applications with long standby periods to prevent battery drain.
faqs.decisionGuide: Sleep mode is automatically entered when no activity detected. Configure wake sources based on application requirements. Contact our FAE team for power optimization.

solutions.title: Automotive Electronics Solution 2

solutions.name: Precision Sensor Interface Solution
solutions.description: Complete signal conditioning solution for precision sensors including strain gauges, thermocouples, and pressure sensors
solutions.longDescription:
The Precision Sensor Interface Solution from PrideSilicon provides a comprehensive signal conditioning platform for industrial and automotive sensing applications. This solution integrates high-precision amplifiers, data converters, and power management components to deliver accurate, reliable sensor measurements in demanding environments.

The solution addresses the complete signal chain from sensor to digital output, including excitation, amplification, filtering, and conversion. Key features include ultra-low noise performance, high CMRR for differential sensors, and ratiometric measurement capability that eliminates errors from reference voltage drift.

Designed for industrial automation, process control, and automotive applications, the solution supports a wide range of sensor types including strain gauges, load cells, thermocouples, RTDs, and pressure sensors. The modular architecture allows customization for specific sensor requirements while maintaining proven performance and reliability.

Comprehensive design support includes reference designs for common sensor types, simulation models, and detailed application notes. LiTong's FAE team provides expert guidance on sensor selection, signal conditioning optimization, and calibration procedures. The solution has been validated in harsh industrial environments and meets automotive qualification requirements.
features: High CMRR: >120dB for excellent noise rejection
Programmable gain: 1-1000x for wide dynamic range

customerCases.result: Delivered safety-certified solution with exceptional field reliability.

faeInsights.title: Senior FAE - Precision Sensor Systems
faeInsights.insight: Precision sensor interface design requires understanding both the sensor characteristics and the application environment. The most common issue I encounter is inadequate attention to the sensor excitation - voltage reference stability directly impacts measurement accuracy. For bridge sensors, always use ratiometric measurement where the ADC reference is derived from the excitation voltage. This eliminates errors from excitation drift. Another critical factor is thermal management - self-heating in the signal conditioning electronics can create temperature gradients that introduce errors. Keep power dissipation low and use good thermal design. For thermocouples, cold junction compensation is often implemented poorly. Use a local temperature sensor mounted near the connection terminals, not on the PCB where temperature may differ. Finally, don't underestimate the importance of shielding and grounding in industrial environments. Proper cable shielding and single-point grounding can make the difference between a working system and one plagued by noise issues.
faeInsights.logic: Precision measurement requires careful attention to every element of the signal chain. The PrideSilicon solution provides optimized components and proven design practices.
keyTakeaways: Use ratiometric measurement for bridge sensors
Use shielding and proper grounding for noise immunity
commonPitfalls: Ignoring lead resistance in remote sensors
bestPractices: Implement ratiometric measurement for all bridge sensors
Use 4-wire Kelvin connections for remote sensors
Place temperature sensors near connection points
Use shielded cables with proper grounding
Minimize power dissipation in signal path
faeInsights.content: Based on extensive field experience with precision sensor applications, this solution addresses the most critical design challenges.

faqs.question: What types of sensors are supported by this solution?
faqs.answer: The Precision Sensor Interface Solution supports a wide range of sensor types including: Strain gauges and load cells for force/weight measurement; Thermocouples (J, K, T, E, N, S, R, B types) for temperature; RTDs (Pt100, Pt1000) for precision temperature; Pressure sensors (piezoresistive, capacitive); Accelerometers and vibration sensors; and Custom bridge-type sensors. The programmable gain and flexible architecture accommodate sensors with output ranges from microvolts to volts.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select sensor type based on measurement requirements. Contact our FAE team for sensor-specific interface recommendations.

faqs.question: How does ratiometric measurement improve accuracy?
faqs.answer: Ratiometric measurement uses the sensor excitation voltage as the ADC reference voltage. Since both sensor output and ADC reference change proportionally with excitation variations, the ratio remains constant. This eliminates errors from excitation voltage drift caused by temperature or loading. For example, if excitation drops by 1%, both sensor output and ADC reference drop by 1%, resulting in zero measurement error. Without ratiometric measurement, a 1% excitation change could cause 1% measurement error.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use ratiometric measurement for bridge sensors. Connect excitation voltage to ADC reference input through appropriate divider.

faqs.question: What is the importance of CMRR in sensor applications?
faqs.answer: CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) is critical for differential sensors like strain gauges and thermocouples. These sensors often operate in noisy environments where both signal lines pick up common-mode noise. High CMRR (>120dB) means the amplifier rejects common-mode noise by a factor of 1,000,000:1. For example, 1V of common-mode noise would produce only 1μV of output error. Without high CMRR, electrical noise from motors, switching power supplies, and radio sources can overwhelm small sensor signals.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use instrumentation amplifiers or precision op-amps with high CMRR for differential sensors. Match source impedances for best CMRR performance.

faqs.question: How do I implement cold junction compensation for thermocouples?
faqs.answer: Cold junction compensation requires measuring the temperature at the thermocouple connection point and adding the equivalent thermocouple voltage for that temperature. Implementation steps: 1) Mount a temperature sensor (thermistor, RTD, or IC sensor) near the thermocouple connection terminals; 2) Measure cold junction temperature with high accuracy; 3) Calculate equivalent thermocouple voltage using standard tables or polynomial; 4) Add cold junction voltage to measured thermocouple voltage; 5) Convert total voltage to temperature using thermocouple characteristics. The reference design includes integrated cold junction compensation with ±0.5°C accuracy.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use integrated cold junction compensation from reference design. Ensure temperature sensor is thermally coupled to connection terminals.

faqs.question: What grounding and shielding practices should I follow?
faqs.answer: Proper grounding and shielding are essential for noise immunity: 1) Use shielded twisted pair cables for sensor connections; 2) Connect cable shield at receiver end only (single-point ground); 3) Use differential signaling for long cable runs; 4) Implement star grounding with separate analog and digital ground planes; 5) Connect ground planes at single point near ADC; 6) Use guard rings around high-impedance inputs; 7) Keep analog traces away from digital signals; 8) Use ferrite beads on power lines for high-frequency filtering. These practices can reduce noise pickup by 40-60dB.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow grounding and shielding guidelines from reference design. Use shielded cables for all sensor connections in industrial environments.

solutions.title: Precision Sensor Interface Solution
